summary:
Blood Fey are the class of Unseelie Fey whose bodies, strength, identity, or supernatural persistence are stabilized by blood-price. They are not simply carnivorous fey, battlefield spirits, vampires, or creatures that enjoy killing. A Blood Fey belongs here when blood is a biological or metaphysical instrument: a token must be kept wet, a garment must be renewed, a wound must remain open, a kill must be witnessed, a challenge must draw blood, or violence itself becomes the fey’s restoring medium.

Redcap-like fey are the anchor example, but the class is broader than redcaps. It may include blood-cap sprites, gore-courtiers, thorn-blood duelists, iron-booted murder fey, slaughter-revel attendants, scarlet bog sprites, and fey whose trophies, clothes, blades, teeth, or names must be fed with blood. The taxon should not absorb every Unseelie predator. If fear is the food, use Frightful Fey. If winter scarcity is the governing law, use Winter Fey. If the creature hunts by quarry-command, use Dark Hunt Fey. If the creature kills because its cap, token, or inherited fey condition demands blood-price, this is the correct class.

A field crew usually identifies Blood Fey by evidence that the blood is being kept rather than merely spilled: caps that remain wet for days, boots that leave red marks on dry stone, blades that clot but never rust, tiny handprints around slaughter sites, red wool in thorn bushes, trophy ribbons that pulse in torchlight, or corpses arranged so their wounds continue feeding a garment, root, charm, or name.
tax:
Blood Fey sit directly beneath Unseelie because blood-price is one of the phylum’s major persistence mechanisms. Where Gloaming Court Fey are stabilized by court recognition and Winter Fey by cold-season law, Blood Fey are stabilized by the exchange of injury for vitality. Blood is not symbolic decoration for them. It is an inherited interface between violence, glamour, and bodily renewal.

This class is separated from ordinary predators by motive and mechanism. A wolf bites because it eats. A vampire drinks because undeath is sustained by stolen life. A demon spills blood because suffering or ruin feeds its lower-planar nature. A Blood Fey may drink, eat, or rejoice in pain, but the important diagnostic feature is fey blood-law: a cap, boot, sash, blade-knot, braid, mask, name, scar, or local rite is strengthened through bloodshed and weakened when the blood-token is denied.

Blood Fey are also separated from Blood Fiends and other fiendish taxa. A fiend’s blood hunger belongs to Outer-Planar evil, soul economy, or lower-planar appetite. Blood Fey remain Inner-Primordial fey: glamour-bound, true-name sensitive, cold-iron vulnerable, and tied to place, taboo, bargain, or story. They can be evil, but evil is not the taxonomic character.

A descendant belongs here only if blood functions as persistence, renewal, jurisdiction, or inherited power. “Red coloration,” “violent culture,” “warrior role,” and “uses bleeding attacks” are insufficient on their own.
ap:
Blood Fey are often easier to smell than to see. Copper, wet wool, old leather, churned mud, iron filings, butcher’s straw, sour wine, and rain on a battlefield are common spoor. Their bodies tend toward compact strength, sharp extremities, visible tendons, oversized hands or feet, hungry mouths, stained teeth, or skin too ruddy, too gray, or too waxy around the lips.

Many are small or stunted, like redcaps: child-height, old-faced, wiry, hunched, long-armed, and stronger than their size suggests. Others are tall and courtly, appearing as duelists, executioners, heralds of blood-debt, or scarlet revelers. Their silhouettes often exaggerate the part of the body that delivers violence: iron-shod feet, hooked fingers, saw-edged nails, overlarge jaws, heavy shoulders, or arms made for weapons too large for the frame.

The diagnostic object is usually more important than the body. A cap may drip without emptying. A sash may darken whenever lies are spoken over a corpse. Boots may ring like iron even on moss. A ribbon may tighten when its owner smells fear-blood. A blade may bead red before it cuts. A mask may blush when a challenge becomes lawful. These tokens are not clothing in the ordinary sense; they are exterior organs of fey persistence.

Field signs include red footprints on clean stone, blood that refuses to dry, thorn branches tied with wet scraps of cloth, corpses shortened by knee or boot strikes, too-large weapon marks near tiny tracks, and laughter or muttering around a body before scavengers arrive.
eco:
Blood Fey gather where violence has become patterned enough for fey nature to learn it: old battlefields, gallows groves, ruined toll roads, clan feud borders, abandoned forts, murder inns, mine strikes, plague pits opened by looters, slaughterhouses near bramble gates, dueling stones, execution yards, and tunnels where blood regularly runs into cracks.

They feed on ordinary food when embodied, often preferring marrow, black bread, mushrooms, sour milk, raw liver, game, roots cooked in fat, or strong drink. Their deeper nourishment comes from blood-price. Some require fresh blood on a token. Some regain vigor after a kill. Some draw strength from wounds they caused. Some need blood spilled under a challenge, insult, ambush, execution, or witnessed feud. Blood shed by accident may be useless to a lineage that requires culpability; battlefield blood may be useless to one that requires personal participation.

Reproduction follows violence and token inheritance. Some Blood Fey breed in stable fey lines, teaching young to make their first token. Some arise when repeated killings stain a fey place until the place produces a body to continue the pattern. Some are lesser Unseelie warped by long service as executioners or blood collectors. Some are born from a failed bargain in which blood was promised instead of coin, apology, or child.

Local populations shrink when the blood economy is interrupted. Cleansed battlefields, reconciled feuds, consecrated gallows, buried victims, cold iron boundary markers, public confession, or ritual destruction of tokens may starve the class from an area. Merely killing one specimen may not be enough if its cap, boot, blade, name, or blood-site remains active.
beh:
Blood Fey are direct compared with court fey, but not mindless. They provoke. They insult. They dare. They test courage, cowardice, and temper because a victim who chooses violence often grants cleaner jurisdiction than one simply ambushed. A Blood Fey may throw stones, cut pack straps, mock the dead, trample food, or strike a sleeping guard’s boot just hard enough to make the camp draw weapons.

Once blood is drawn, restraint declines quickly. Many become joyful, even childish, around open wounds. Some caper and sing. Some lecture the dying about etiquette. Some become solemn, treating the renewal of a token as a sacrament. Some argue over who earned the blood, because not every drop has equal force. Blood from a foe the fey helped kill may matter more than blood from a carcass; blood taken under challenge may matter more than blood stolen from a sleeping victim.

They are often territorial around sites of previous violence. A bridge where a toll-keeper was murdered, a hall where guests were slaughtered, or a mine gallery where workers died in a strike may be treated as a pantry, shrine, nursery, and court all at once. Other Unseelie use them as enforcers, but this is dangerous. Blood Fey obey bargains while the bargain is fresh; old blood, insult, or a better opportunity can pull them out of formation.

They hate being denied escalation. A party that refuses dares, binds wounds immediately, retreats without insult, conceals blood-scent, or ritually names the dead may frustrate them more effectively than a party that simply fights hard.
traitsText:
Type: Fey.

Senses: Low-light vision. Scent is common in descendant taxa that track wounds, but it should be added locally rather than assumed for the entire class. Darkvision may appear in underground or nocturnal lines.

Languages: Sylvan is standard. Aklo, Common, Goblin, Giant, Undercommon, and local feud-cants may appear in lower taxa. Some lesser Blood Fey speak mostly through threats, laughter, counting rhymes, or repeated challenge phrases.

Proficiencies: As fey: simple weapons and any weapons named in the descendant entry. Blood Fey commonly favor scythes, sickles, cleavers, knives, oversized blades, iron-shod boots, hooked spears, pins, garrotes, and improvised trophies. Armor proficiency applies only when the creature is described as wearing armor.

Defensive Traits: Common descendant defenses include DR/cold iron, fast healing tied to a blood-token, morale bonuses after causing bleed damage, bonuses against fear while bloodied, or temporary resilience while wearing a renewed token. These are not universal and should be assigned to lower taxa.

Weaknesses: Cold iron is common. Other recurring weaknesses include removal or destruction of the blood-token, drying or cleansing the token, consecration, salt, public naming of the victim, denied challenge, divine symbols of mercy or protection, and inability to renew after a bloodless defeat. Redcap-style irreligious revulsion is appropriate for some descendants but should not be copied to the whole class.

Skills: Intimidate, Perception, Stealth, Survival, Acrobatics, Climb, Escape Artist, and Sense Motive are common skill directions. Descendant taxa may receive racial bonuses to track bleeding creatures, hide in old battlefields, intimidate wounded enemies, or move in oversized boots or armor.

Environment: Temperate forests, hills, mountains, ruins, underground, battlefields, gallows sites, feud borders, abandoned forts, murder roads, and First World/Feywild borderlands.

Organization: Solitary, pair, gang, blood-band, feud-clutch, murder-revel, execution retinue, or token-line.
abilities:
Blood-Token Renewal (Su). Many Blood Fey bind their vigor to a cap, boot, sash, braid, blade-knot, mask, ribbon, or trophy kept wet or ritually marked with blood. Descendant entries may express this as fast healing, bonuses on damage rolls, morale bonuses, temporary hit points, DR, or restored uses of a special ability. The token should have counterplay: removal, destruction, drying, cleansing, salting, consecration, cold iron contact, or public naming of the victim whose blood empowers it.

Red Cap Inheritance (Su). Redcap-like descendants may specifically require a blood-soaked cap. Pathfinder’s redcap uses a cap that grants a damage bonus and fast healing while worn, loses those benefits if the cap is removed or destroyed, and must create and blood the replacement before it functions. Use this as the model for cap-bound Blood Fey, but do not give every Blood Fey an actual cap.

Blood Soak (Su). A Blood Fey may renew a token by dipping, pressing, dragging, or steeping it in the blood of a creature it helped wound, defeat, kill, execute, or lawfully challenge. Lower taxa should define the action required, the time limit after death or injury, the duration of the benefit, and whether animal, mortal, fey, divine, or unwilling blood counts. The ability should not trigger from arbitrary stored blood unless that lineage explicitly allows it.

Gore-Fed Ferocity (Ex or Su). Some Blood Fey become more dangerous as nearby creatures bleed. This may appear as bonuses on attack rolls, damage rolls, Intimidate checks, saving throws against fear, or movement toward wounded targets. The ability should be bounded by range, visible blood, scent, or a creature the fey personally injured. It should not become universal battlefield omniscience.

Wound-Scent (Ex). Descendants with this ability can track fresh blood, concealed wounds, battlefield trails, butchered meat, or recently cleaned murder sites. This is usually modeled as scent with a blood-specific bonus, or as a racial bonus on Survival checks to track wounded creatures. It should fail or suffer penalties against bloodless creatures, sealed wounds, strong alchemical masking, running water, or consecrated cleansing.

Iron-Boot Violence (Ex). Redcap-like and boot-bound descendants may use heavy iron boots, spiked soles, or unnatural kicking strength as part of movement or full attacks. Pathfinder’s redcap has a boot-stomp style ability and heavy iron boots used to deadly effect. Lower taxa should define whether the attack is a secondary natural attack, a special movement attack, a trip rider, bleed damage, or a bonus against prone targets.

Heavy Weapon Impudence (Ex). Some small Blood Fey wield weapons too large for their size without the usual penalty, echoing the redcap’s oversized scythe tradition. This should remain local to stocky, brutal, or weapon-fixated descendants. It is not appropriate for delicate blood-sprites, court duelists, or blood-drinking lurkers unless their anatomy supports it.

Challenge-Hook (Su). A Blood Fey may gain supernatural permission from insult, dare, duel, feud, trespass, or refusal to pay blood-price. Descendant expressions may allow a bonus against the challenger, a curse for backing down, or activation of a blood-token after first blood. The hook must be clear enough for counterplay. Silence, formal refusal, apology, lawful payment, or a witness declaring the challenge void may deny it.

Bleed the Story (Su). Stronger Blood Fey can make an old act of violence present again. A battlefield may reopen old wounds, a murder room may sweat red, or a dueling stone may impose penalties on creatures descended from the original feud. This ability should be tied to a known site or story and should have a release condition: burial, confession, restitution, consecration, reconciliation, or destruction of the token anchoring the event.

Irreligious Revulsion (Ex or Su). Some Blood Fey, especially redcap-like descendants, recoil from symbols of good-aligned mercy, protection, or consecrated burial. Pathfinder redcaps have a specific reaction to presented good-aligned holy symbols. Use this only for lineages whose blood-price is explicitly blasphemous or anti-sanctuary; do not apply it automatically to all Blood Fey.

Bloodless Frustration (Ex). Blood Fey may lose tactical discipline when denied bloodshed. Descendant entries can express this as penalties after several rounds without drawing blood, compulsion to attack a bleeding target, or loss of token benefits if wounds are immediately sealed. This weakness is useful because it makes non-escalation, healing, restraint, and battlefield control meaningful counters.
bg:
Blood Fey matter because they turn violence into ecology. A red footprint is not just evidence of a fight; it may be evidence that something has been fed. A wet cap, clanging iron boot, oversized scythe mark, or corpse arranged to keep bleeding tells a field crew that the creature’s power has an external handle. The correct response is not only “bring stronger weapons,” but “find the token, stop the renewal, name the dead, clean the site, and do not give the fey the challenge it wants.”

For route-makers, shrine-keepers, Pall-Brokers, and village elders, Blood Fey are especially dangerous in places where violence is socially repeated: feud borders, war roads, execution grounds, abandoned forts, and mines where old killings were never settled. Every new death may refresh more than grief. It may maintain a local population.

This class also keeps the taxonomy from misfiling redcap-like beings as goblins, fiends, undead, or generic “evil small folk.” Their cruelty can look demonic and their blood hunger can look vampiric, but their actual nature is fey: glamour-bound, token-bound, cold-iron vulnerable, etiquette-sensitive, and sustained by a story of blood rather than by lower-planar evil or necromantic undeath.
